figures.caption: Table 1: Scaling factors for different strategies and post-training regimes.
Figure 8: Conditional Scaling. BoN-aligned models exhibit superior test-time scaling properties compared to the Standard RL baseline.
Figure 7: Unconditional Scaling. Models trained with BoN objectives exhibit superior scaling behavior at test time.

Figure 18: Maj@64 Hyperparameter Sweep. Performance delta relative to SFT. The loose threshold (25%, Blue) fails to improve over the baseline, while the stricter threshold (40%, Green) recovers performance.
Table 11: Hyperparameters for Pass@N GRPO Training.
Figure 1: Pass@k Improvement over SFT. The performance difference (Pass@kmodel − Pass@kSFT). High-N models (Purple) sacrifice low-budget accuracy to achieve superior performance at scale.
Table 12: Hyperparameters for Majority Vote RL Experiments.

Figure 10: Empirical distribution of optimization efficiency (ρ) for the Best-of-N diagonal approximation (N = 8,M = 64) across generated protein sequences. The empirical mean of ρ ≈ 1.26 (green solid line) demonstrates that the computationally efficient diagonal approximation doesn’t wildly overestimate the true signal.

Table 14: Ablation Results: SFT vs. RL Weights. Comparison of Majority Vote accuracy (Maj@k) across different weighting strategies and training budgets. The RL_Wt estimators generally provide superior scaling at higher inference budgets compared to their SFT_Wt counterparts, confirming that the "safety brake" normalization in SFT weights may dampen the critical signal required for consensus.

Figure 19: Scaling Laws for RL Weighting Strategies. The Log-Weighted estimator at N = 16 avoids the optimization instability of the Pure RL estimator, allowing the model to successfully translate a higher training budget into superior test-time scaling.
Figure 6: Conditional Shift. Density of Output vs. Input. BoN models stretch to align with the ideal identity line (red dashed) better than the clustered baseline.
Table 10: Training hyperparameters for Test-Time Strategy experiments.

Figure 9: Empirical distribution of optimization efficiency (ρ) for the diagonal approximation under Majority Vote (N = 8) across the MATH validation subset. The distribution strictly obeys the theoretical lower bound of ρ ≥ 0.5 (red dashed line) with an empirical mean of ρ ≈ 0.863 (green solid line). This demonstrates that the off-diagonal rivalry terms account for less than 14% of the optimization signal on average, allowing the computationally efficient diagonal approximation to preserve the vast majority of the true gradient magnitude.

Table 13: Full comparison of RL models trained with different weighting strategies. SFT-derived (Log) weights consistently yield better scaling properties than pure RL-derived weights by preventing variance explosion during GRPO updates.
Figure 2: Comparison of Best Models vs SFT. All models demonstrate strong test time scaling, confirming that CAT modifies the distribution to support aggregation.

Figure 20: Full Scaling Laws (All Models). A comparison of Majority Vote accuracy across inference budgets k. The strategy-aware models (colored lines) consistently outperform the Standard RL baseline (black line) at higher budgets. Notably, the RL_Wt_Maj4 model (Green) achieves the steepest scaling curve, demonstrating that the raw marginal utility estimator provides the strongest signal for consensus optimization.

Figure 17: The Support Mismatch: Side-by-side visualization of strategy-aware SFT scaling factors w(p) compared to the uniform gradient pressure of Standard SFT (Gray dashed line). Left (Pass@N): The objective acts as an efficiency regularizer, exposing a massive "Waste Region" where standard SFT unnecessarily optimizes already-solved problems (the "SFT Tax"). Right (Majority Vote): The objective concentrates gradient utility sharply at the decision boundary (p ≈ k/N), revealing a "Starvation Region" where SFT fails to provide sufficient localized pressure to tip the consensus. Across both strategies, the shaded non-overlapping regions represent fundamentally misallocated gradient.
Figure 4: Majority Vote RL Scaling. The CAT models significantly outperform the baseline at higher inference budgets.
Figure 3: Pass@k RL Scaling. Models trained with strategy-aware objectives (Pass@16) scale better than the baseline.
